message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/
**Title:** Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – an fMRI study (Dietrich et al., 2013, BMC Neuroscience 14:74)
**Found via:** Training/plasticity in older adults & blindness onset (early- vs late-blind ultrafast speech) search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all findings relevant to: (1) ultra-fast / time-compressed speech comprehension in blind vs sighted subjects, including syllables/second rates achieved; (2) how AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind / congenital vs late) affects ultra-fast speech comprehension ability and neural (visual cortex / V1) plasticity; (3) any quantitative figures on speech rates (syllables per second, words per minute, % time compression), age of subjects, group sizes; (4) which brain regions (primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, pulvinar) are engaged and how this relates to blindness onset; (5) any mention of comprehension thresholds, performance differences between groups. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding. Also note the publication date and study type (primary research vs review).

content.content:
# Key Findings on Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Blind Subjects

**Publication:** BMC Neuroscience, July 23, 2013 (Primary Research)

## 1. ULTRA-FAST SPEECH COMPREHENSION RATES

**Blind vs. Sighted Performance:**
- Blind subjects achieved up to 22 syllables/second: "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second"
- Sighted maximum ~8 syl/s: "exceeding by far the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)"

**Study Speech Rates:**
- Moderately fast: 8 syl/s
- Ultra-fast: 16 syl/s
- Both conditions: ~3.78-3.84 seconds duration per sentence

**Performance Data (n=14 blind, n=12 sighted):**
- Ultra-fast comprehension in blind: "extended from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)"
- Sighted controls: "performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)"
- Significant group difference: "blind and sighted individuals significantly differed with respect to speech comprehension of ultra-fast test materials (two-sample t-test; T = 7.74, p < .001)"

---

## 2. AGE OF ONSET EFFECTS ON SPEECH COMPREHENSION

**Primary Finding - No Significant Correlation:**
"The enhanced perceptual skills of blind listeners did not show a significant correlation with either the onset of vision loss (r = -.347, p = .224) or with the duration of blindness"

**Study Composition:**
- Early-blind (n=3): congenital or onset age 1-14 years
- Late-blind (n=11): onset after age 14
- Sample sizes deemed "too small for any meaningful statistical comparisons"

**Behavioral Performance Independence:**
"hemodynamic responses to moderately fast verbal utterances did not show any significant correlations with ultra-fast speech comprehension capabilities in blind listeners...apart from left aSTS"

---

## 3. NEURAL PLASTICITY & VISUAL CORTEX ENGAGEMENT

**Overall Brain Activation Pattern:**
"blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv)"

**Lateralization Finding:**
"right hemisphere comprised significantly stronger hemodynamic responses than the left side (factor Hemisphere: F (1, 12) = 10.43, p < .01)"

**Early vs. Late-Blind Differences:**
Early-blind subjects showed "more extensive and a more bilateral distribution" of occipital responses, but post-hoc analysis found "no significant lateralization effect at the group level (T = -1.68, p = .218)"

**Age of Onset & Plasticity:**
"ultra-fast speech perception does not appear to depend upon major rewiring of visual cortex...rather, this perceptual capability seems associated with task-dependent cross-modal functional plasticity"

**Correlation with Performance:**
"BOLD signal changes within these two areas showed a positive correlation with individual ultra-fast speech perception skills" (V1: r = .533, p < .05; FG: r = .650, p < .05)

---

## 4. BRAIN REGIONS ENGAGED

**Primary Visual Cortex (V1):**
- Right hemisphere dominant activation
- Involvement correlates with behavioral performance
- Processing suprasegmental/prosodic information: "V1 might support...the encoding of early suprasegmental aspects of the speech signal"

**Fusiform Gyrus (FG):**
- Left hemisphere, contralateral to V1 activation
- Role: "FG supports the left-hemispheric perisylvian 'language network'...during the (segmental) sequencing of verbal utterances"
- Functions in phonological processing

**Pulvinar (Pv):**
- Bilateral activation
- Role: "Pv might help to synchronize...striate cortex with the central-auditory system during ultra-fast speech perception"

**Additional Regions:**
- Left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG)
- Bilateral posterior superior temporal sulcus (pSTS)
- Left supplementary motor area (SMA)

---

## 5. COMPREHENSION THRESHOLDS & PERFORMANCE DIFFERENCES

**Group-Level Threshold:**
Seven blind subjects with >60% ultra-fast comprehension showed consistent V1 activation within the V1 anatomical mask during "ultra-fast versus baseline" condition

**Sighted Control Response:**
"sighted group did not show any significant correlations between the hemodynamic responses obtained under the two speech conditions and ultra-fast speech comprehension capabilities"

**Moderately Fast Speech:**
"Moderately fast utterances (8 syl/s) yielded comparable results in both subject groups (range across all participants: 61 to 100%; sighted controls: mean = 80.8%, SD ± 11.8%; blind listeners: mean = 84.1%, SD ± 11.0%)"

**No Significant Difference at Moderate Rates:**
"two-sample t-test; T = 0.74, p = .470"

content.text: I have comprehensive content. Let me extract the falsifiable claims most relevant to the research question, particularly angle (5) on blindness onset modulating ultra-fast speech advantage.

claims.claim: Trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at up to 22 syllables/second, versus ~8 syl/s for untrained sighted subjects; in this study blind subjects achieved a mean 59.7% comprehension of ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech (range 0-93%) while sighted controls fell below 16% (mean 9.0%), a highly significant difference (T=7.74, p<.001).
claims.quote: repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second... exceeding by far the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)... blind and sighted individuals significantly differed with respect to speech comprehension of ultra-fast test materials (two-sample t-test; T = 7.74, p < .001)

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension ability in blind listeners did NOT correlate significantly with age of onset of vision loss or duration of blindness; early-blind (n=3) vs late-blind (n=11) groups were too small for meaningful statistical comparison.
claims.quote: The enhanced perceptual skills of blind listeners did not show a significant correlation with either the onset of vision loss (r = -.347, p = .224) or with the duration of blindness

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engaged right primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, with V1 and FG activation positively correlated with individual comprehension skill (V1: r=.533, p<.05; FG: r=.650, p<.05).
claims.quote: blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv)... BOLD signal changes within these two areas showed a positive correlation with individual ultra-fast speech perception skills

claims.claim: The visual-cortex recruitment underlying ultra-fast speech in blind subjects reflects task-dependent cross-modal functional plasticity rather than major structural rewiring, though early-blind subjects showed more extensive and bilateral occipital responses than late-blind subjects.
claims.quote: ultra-fast speech perception does not appear to depend upon major rewiring of visual cortex...rather, this perceptual capability seems associated with task-dependent cross-modal functional plasticity

claims.claim: At moderately fast speech (8 syl/s) blind and sighted groups performed comparably (blind mean 84.1%, sighted 80.8%; T=0.74, p=.470), so the blind advantage is specific to ultra-fast rates rather than a general speech-comprehension difference.
claims.quote: Moderately fast utterances (8 syl/s) yielded comparable results in both subject groups... sighted controls: mean = 80.8%, SD ± 11.8%; blind listeners: mean = 84.1%, SD ± 11.0%... two-sample t-test; T = 0.74, p = .470

content.text:
Extracted from Dietrich et al. (2013, BMC Neuroscience 14:74), a primary fMRI study (n=14 blind, n=12 sighted). Key relevance to the research question's angle (5):

- Blind training can push comprehension to ~22 syl/s vs ~8 syl/s untrained — directly contextualizing the reference subject at ~13 syl/s as well within trained-blind range but already above untrained-sighted limits.
- Crucially, age of onset of blindness did NOT significantly correlate with ultra-fast comprehension skill (r=-.347, p=.224), though the early-blind subgroup (n=3) was too small for robust comparison — a flag worth noting.
- Neural plasticity (V1, fusiform, pulvinar recruitment) was described as task-dependent functional cross-modal plasticity, not structural rewiring, with early-blind showing more bilateral occipital responses.

Note: this study does not address aging/presbycusis or developmental curves (angles 1-4); it bears almost exclusively on angle 5 (blindness onset and plasticity). All subjects were adults; peripheral hearing was not the focus (blind vs sighted matched on moderate-rate speech, controlling for general audibility/comprehension).
